item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm forced to admit that there are a slightly silly number of SCPs that are just a parallel universe leaking into our own somehow.

I'm forced to acknowledge this fact and feel like a fraud for writing 6 extradimensional articles

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, a substantial majority of SCPs aren't huge scary eldritch things, especially once you get out of Series 1.

… I'm forced to admit that there are a slightly silly number of SCPs that are just a parallel universe leaking into our own somehow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one. It's a really nice example of a small scale anomaly. Not every SCP has to be world ending eldritch horror or yet another bloody parallel universe (how many do we have already?).

Besides, this has a really chilling and human basis, a young girl with he arm rotting off + there are more items turning up. it feels a lot more real than most of the SCPs not to mention it's extremely well written. It deserves +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If handled well that first interview could have settled my vote on neutral. However, it felt honestly rushed and not matching up to how intense that moment should have been (I don't mind agents losing their composture, but the transition there is really awkward).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted. It has that distinct quality of an old, creepy SCP. It has some weird blackboxing and a few clichés as well, but that does not make it a -1 for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted, but the expungements are bad, like real bad: A lot of things that shouldn't ever be catalogued as classified are blackboxed here, this really lessens any effect the scip would have.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember this being one of the first new scips I ever saw. Still like it, despite its flaws.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Expungement everywhere, bad tone, awkward dialogue, boring concept of gloves that kill you when you wear them, and that note from the researcher makes this a -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Basically, what everyone else has been saying, especially in regards to the grandmother - the Foundation would almost certainly at least interview the grandmother in regards to when and how she went about knitting the mitten, even if other, unrelated instances of the scip are appearing; they'd want to exhaust all possibilities. And yes, there's too much expungement. Also, what would the human body be doing to counteract the effects of the glove during the first two hours? Is the glove introducing new pathogens, or is it just an issue of the body working extra-hard to replace dying cells?

There's something else you mention at one point but never take anywhere that especially bothered me: The effects of the glove on nonliving, non-human, or under-resilient-human matter. It was so brief, I honestly don't even remember off the top of my head whether or not the effects were under a "[DATA EXPUNGED]". Please go somewhere with that, or get rid of it; it's the kind of thing that you allude to halfway through an SCP article, then save for the end to explain, and it's usually the final clincher for the whole article - it could bring this article so much further.

Overall, the article has kind of a half-baked feel to me, like it isn't complete yet; elaborate on the grandmother, the effects, and even a few of the expungements, and I'm sure that would be fixed. Also, remove the second interview; it's disturbing, but ultimately pointless and redundant.

That said, thinking about the effects of this thing did make me physically shudder, so, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, gloves that rot you don't really do anything for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, I have to downvote this one for multiple tone issues.

To clarify:
1. It reads too much like a narrative, and not enough like a write up of a dangerous item. This article reads like "The Tragic Death of Sally Blackbox", which doesn't feel appropriate for the site.

2. There are too many wishy-washy, weedling words or phrases for a scientific report. "probably" "usually" "shortly thereafter" (That last one also adds to the "narrative" feel in point 1.)

3. The [DATA EXPUNGED] in the stages of progression seems extremely forced and entirely unnecessary. You're more or less describing the process of decomposing, necrotizing flesh. What really needs to be Expunged? Aliens come down, shake hands with 05s, and sick Abel on the offending body part? It reminds me too much of that one meme: Step one: Decreaded white cells. Step Two: Fungal Growth. Step Three: ????? Step Four: Profit.

4. I didn't actually finish the article. I'm sorry. I had to stop at "Despite heroic livesaving attempts". If ever there was a phrase I didn't expect to see on this site…. This isn't an news paper editorial, and trying to get me to care about Sally Blackbox so hard just gave me the opposite reaction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this thing's effect viral? Bacterial? Fungal? Unless it involves foreign objects, the human immune system generally won't do anything to stop it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with Lexagon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't agree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) Sure there is. It gives you the vague range while obscuring the exact details, which there's plenty of in-universe reason for.

2) It's the ramblings of a distressed little girl, and even if the grandmother knitted it, the existence of other instances with an unconnected source demonstrate that that's probably now how it gain its anomalous properties.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two things:

1) "…observed in 7█% of test subjects…"

There's no point in boxing the second digit of a two digit number, it obscures nothing of import and looks silly.

2) "…Grandma made it…"

You can't give the origin of an anomalous item like this and not explore it at least a little. Well, I guess you can, but it stands out in a Foundation report as quite odd that you haven't.

Other than that, nasty little item. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This feels like - and you've commented that it is the case - expungement and redaction is being used to cover up a lack of ideas. That really shows through; you want to lead the reader along far enough that they 'know' something horrible is coming, set it up…and then the redaction hits, leaving them to imagine the worst.

Here, it's being used for "Scary stuff here!" but you haven't set up anything scary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Between 10 and ██ hours of exposure Abnormal fungal growth has been observed in 7█% of test subjects at this stage. In particular, the affected areas have been known to [DATA EXPUNGED] unexpected development of parasites, especially [REDACTED]

Between ██ and ██ hours of exposure [DATA EXPUNGED]

More than 24 hours of exposure Almost total necrosis of affected regions. By this point, the combined effects of septic shock and [DATA EXPUNGED] will have all but ensured the death of the subject. █ subjects have survived late-stage SCP-1035 exposure for as long as ██ days following extraordinary lifesaving work by medical personnel. Amputation is effective in preventing other body parts from experiencing the effects of SCP-1035. Requests to test SCP-1035 in conjunction with SCP-500 and other medical-based SCPs have been denied.

Downvoted because you seem to have a bit of an expungement fetish, blotting out and censoring things that it makes no sense to blot out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah. Good catch with the first one. As for the second, I'm not quite as sure if I'll change that. Maybe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not entirely sure about the editing rules here for things that are clearly typos or something, and I'm too tired to look them up now, so I'm just gonna tell you about it instead of fixing it myself.

On the table that lists symptoms based on how many hours worn, you have one for "Between 10 and █ hours of exposure." The blacked out number is clearly a single digit. Unless there's a temporal anomaly here as well, I think you may want to add another "█" to that number.

On a less-technical note, I have to agree with the person who said they disliked "19██" as a date. I don't need an exact year, but a specific decade might be nice. I don't mind so much with "20██," but only because we're not that many years into it.

But that's just my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, just curious.

(This was not meant to be a side-eye or an oblique accusation, btw. Just a, "Hey, this very vaguely reminds me of a thing I read once; wonder if the author was thinking of it, too.")

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Er, I don't think so. I think I actually took more inspiration from Terry Pratchett's Making Money. You know the scene.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah. Thanks. I will update at first possible opportunity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was this at all inspired by The Replacement?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would like to know more about the place Grandma bought her material. Maybe the packaging says "The Factory" on it.

If you wanted this to sound more medical, you could use the term serous for clear fluid, and serosanguinous for blood-tinged clear fluid. Blood poisoning is an old term , and usually sepsis is used nowadays. Ultimately your subject would die of septic shock from the various infections. You could put in whether the infections are susceptible to antibiotics, anthelmintic (for parasites) or antifungal agents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to know more about the grandma that made it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Going to cautiously upvote; I see some good potential here, maybe a bit of clean-up as most people put above. Well written overall and a good core, but the whole "Object mysteriously gives you super-fast-acting condition" typically doesn't do it for me as just a matter of personal preference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sufficiently horrible! Maybe we need to contain the grandma who made these.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's alright, just alright. I can see you're going for the "innocent looking but deadly" thing with the mittens but I'm not feeling it really. The idea is good though, so I can see it turning into a fine SCP with some improvement. Also I noticed some spelling and grammer errors so you might want to fix those.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The mittens work nicely. I'd ditch the second interview and keep the first; the second is pretty redundant. I also echo Anqxyr's point about excessive expungement. A case of necrotizing fasciitis, for example, isn't something the Foundation would classify, nor is it too disgusting for this site's readership. Go ahead and put it in.

In the future, if you're having trouble thinking of something sufficiently horrible, don't just expunge it. That rarely works very well. Ask the chat instead: there are experienced writers here who can help you fine-tune your expungement, and there are certainly enough depraved minds to help you come up with something cringeworthy. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Believe it or not, "heroic" is a technical term, and it means exactly the right thing here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, just added a nice photo of pink wool mittens, plus two interviews. If people like it, they'll stay. If everyone starts downvoting, I'll switch it back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I simply like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
heroic efforts on the part of the Foundation’s medical staff.

I wouldn't put heroic. I think it be better if it was "Extreme" or "Highly skilled". Something to show that they were able to save the subject ,but at a cost. Maybe amputation, maybe damage of the brain. Who knows?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely find an image, or several. Don't know why I didn't suggest it. Must be lack of sleep. But, yeah, everything is better with images. Even 1012 have one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I might find a few nice images somewhere. Also will think of expanding the timespan. Interview in progress. Updates to this article should occur sometime today.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
how she wants her arm to be whole again.

And this pushed me to upvote. Now you need to make so that mental image I just had would be more visible from the description. No idea how you do that. Interview may improve the article and may make it worse, depending on how well you'll pull it off.

Maybe you should also increase the timespan, to, say, two-three month, with necrosis slowly propagating through skin and muscle tissue, but without affecting organs until later stage, so that Sally and her parents will have more time to savor the effects. You can have different time intervals for "subject was wearing the mitten too long, effect is now irreversible" and "necrosis is fully manifested, subject will die soon". 24-hours seems alright for the first one. Also, subjects in later stages, when muscle tissue is already gone, but organs don't affected for couple of weeks yet, may die from starvation if they live alone and no one else knows that something is happening to them.

So, yes, you have a room for improvement, use it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi. Thanks for feedback.

To be completely honest, I couldn't really think of anything all that disgusting when I hit a certain point in designing the chart, so I just expunged some of the data and left it at that. I thought that maybe someone else's imagination could think up a more horrifying sequence of events to plug the gaps, preferably involving blowfly larvae and gangrene. Maybe I will elaborate a little…

Same thing with the project name. Of course, maybe it's just secret enough that hiding what it's called is required, but the Foundation isn't particularly concerned with low-level employees knowing that they've got someone working on the thing. If I ever think up a good name, I'll change that.

For the disease: I was thinking of necrotizing fasciitis when I was writing the cover story. Didn't want to go and say it outright, so I redacted that part. According to Wikipedia, Herod the Great may have had the thing in his groin regions. Isn't that a lovely way to die.

I see what you mean about the specific date. I will edit that ASAP.

If there's really a large amount of demand for it, I may add an interview log or something with young Sally ██████ and have her tell the doctor/embedded agent about how she wants her arm to be whole again. I'm not sure how it'll turn out, so unless loads of people really want to see me try I won't do it yet.

I'll argue that the item is a little more detailed than a simple "oh god it kills you in horrible ways" but that's really up to opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not the most interesting SCP I have read, but well-written and a good first effort…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From "How To Write An SCP" guide:

a thing that will liquefy bone in a quarter-mile radius is very dangerous, and would need special containment, but it’s not interesting.

This is too close to that for me to upvote.

However, writing and tone are unusually good for a first-try SCP, so I can't downvote it either. My only nitpick is a little too heavy use of expungement. For example:

It is difficult to determine the precise date … effects first began at or around █ February, 19██.

Knowing the date is pretty precise. You can just say "around February, 19██", or drop the "difficult to determine" part and state exact date. Also, I hate 19██-like dates and would suggest that you just pick a date instead of blackboxing it, but that's mostly my personal reaction and other people may not agree with me on this.

Another example:

involving a case of necrotizing [REDACTED].

Again, just pick a disease that causes necrosis and use it instead of [REDACTED]. Also, my spellchecker says that necrotizing isn't a word, but he says many things and I don't usually trust him.

And again, from the table:

Between █ and ██ hours of exposure [DATA EXPUNGED]

If you have a whole section with the only content been [DATA EXPUNGED], you have a problem with expungement.

And yet again:

The [REDACTED] Project

Just pick a name and stick with it. On average, the less expungements article has, the stronger effect and meaning each of them have.

I don't know how to vote on this. Writing is mostly good, but concept is bland. I don't like to no-vote articles, but I just may have to in this case, unless you somehow expand the idea into something more interesting, so that I can upvote without feeling guilty.

item.title: First SCP
item.content:encoded: This is my first stab at writing an article for this site. If there's anything missing, something that should be added, something that just isn't right, or if the article is just plain wrong about some detail, let me know and I'll try to fix it. Thank you.
